policy_statement: Revise State procurement specifications to expand eligibility for qualifying wood products and set minimum standards for wood use in new State-funded construction and infrastructure projects as feasible.
mechanism_description: Revising State procurement specifications and setting minimum standards for wood use in State-funded construction increases adoption of qualifying wood products, which can reduce embodied emissions from construction materials.
primary_causal_pathway: Procurement/material standards → Material substitution in construction → ↓ embodied (lifecycle) GHG emissions (+ potential biogenic carbon storage) → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Changed activity/system: Public-sector procurement and design specifications for State-funded construction are revised to set/encourage minimum use of qualifying wood products. (2) Mediating process: Material substitution reduces upstream industrial emissions from producing cement/steel and related supply-chain energy use; in some cases, harvested wood products can store biogenic carbon over the building lifetime (subject to sourcing and end-of-life assumptions). (3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower embodied/lifecycle GHG emissions per building/infrastructure asset, contributing to overall emissions reductions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing embodied greenhouse gas emissions from construction by substituting lower-embodied-carbon (and potentially carbon-storing) wood products for more emissions-intensive materials.
mechanism_classification_reasoning: 1) Dominant causal pathway: Procurement rules drive construction material substitution, yielding lower embodied/lifecycle GHG emissions—this is a direct emissions reduction pathway. 2) Why this primary over alternatives: While there can be resource-efficiency co-benefits (less emissions per unit service) and possible carbon storage in wood, the dominant intent and impact is GHG mitigation via reduced embodied emissions. 3) Edge cases applied: If “wood standards” primarily aimed at climate resilience (e.g., fire-resistant materials) or at waste reduction, the primary could shift, but the stated mechanism is explicitly embodied-emissions reduction through wood adoption. If wood is sourced unsustainably or causes land-use change, net mitigation benefits could be reduced, but the mechanism category remains mitigation. 4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy using procurement/standards to increase qualifying wood use to reduce embodied construction emissions shares the same primary mitigation pathway. 5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., industrial decarbonization vs. buildings) depends on policy-level evidence and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Net mitigation depends on life-cycle assumptions (sustainable forestry, additionality, end-of-life treatment, and displacement of steel/cement). Some implementations may emphasize carbon storage vs. avoided industrial emissions, but both remain within mitigation.
key_indicators: "Revise State procurement specifications"; "set minimum standards for use of wood products"; "new State-funded construction and infrastructure projects"; "limit the eligibility of wood products"; "technical performance standards"
co_benefits: Potential economic development for forestry/wood-products sector; potential waste reduction/material circularity benefits (if paired with reuse); possible indoor air quality/health benefits depending on materials used.
instance_edge_case_notes: “As feasible” introduces implementation discretion (could soften enforceability), but the presence of “set minimum standards” and procurement specification changes still supports classification as a procurement requirement with direct mitigation relevance.
